Explore This Site

About Me

The story behind the work and the why behind the purpose

My Story

I did not arrive at governance and compliance through a straightforward path, and I think that is exactly what makes my perspective useful.

I have always worked full time and studied. During the early part of my career I was studying Marketing and International Business. Then I did an introductory law unit, and it pulled me drawn to the way legislation could be read, reasoned through, and applied to real situations. Then life took over. I paused to raise my family, but the pull toward law never left.

I returned to complete my Bachelor of Laws in Business at Edith Cowan University this time with 20 years of industry experience sitting behind every case I read and every regulatory framework I analysed. That combination of lived practice and formal study is not something you can manufacture. It is earned.

Over two decades in Australian financial services, I have worked across mortgage broking operations, compliance, risk, and governance at a senior level. I have served as Responsible Manager under an Australian Credit Licence, led the design and delivery of compliance frameworks under the NCCP Act and Best Interest Duty, and managed complex investigations. I have prepared Board-ready papers, ASIC-aligned documentation, and ASX-level reporting. I know what it means to operate where the stakes are real.

But what I am most proud of is not the frameworks I built. It is the people I helped along the way watching a broker move from employee to business owner, seeing audit pass rates climb from 50% to 100%, supporting women in the industry who had never had someone in their corner before.

I am a Golden Key International Honour Society member, consecutive Dean's Letter recipient, and MFAA State Winner and National Finalist. I am also the developer of an AML Compliance Framework and AI Governance System for settlement agents an area of the property market often overlooked, with a majority of women as single or small operators who deserve frameworks that are technically rigorous and genuinely human.

Studying law later in life has also broadened my understanding of justice in ways I did not expect opening my eyes to systemic inequities facing Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples under Western law. These are not abstract issues for me. They have deepened my conviction that good governance is not just technical. It is profoundly ethical.

Passions & Advocacy

Beyond my professional work, there are causes I hold close areas where I believe I have both a responsibility and an opportunity to make a difference.

Women in Leadership and Financial Services

I am passionate about developing women in mortgage broking and supporting the transition from employee to business owner. I have watched the gender pay gap persist and watched the "lean in" narrative give way to burnout feminism, where women are simply too exhausted to keep pushing against structures that were never designed for them. This is not a pipeline problem. It is a systems problem. And those of us with a platform have an obligation to say so.

Mental Health in Real Estate and Broking

The suicide rate among men is something I think about seriously, especially in the industries I have come from: real estate and mortgage broking. These are high-pressure, commission-driven environments where vulnerability is rarely welcomed and asking for help can feel like professional risk. I advocate for better support systems for men not as a gesture, but as a structural priority.

Domestic Violence and Women's Safety

This cause is one I have put into practice, not just spoken about. I co-designed FDV workplace support policies, launched She Knows a women's network that grew to over 130 members established a privacy-first DV committee, and secured specialist training with Zonta House. Financial independence is one of the most powerful tools a woman can have.

AI Governance and Ethics

The rapid integration of AI into workplaces is one of the most pressing global challenges particularly around harm, regulatory gaps, and the need for ethical frameworks that protect consumers and workers. This is not a future problem. It is already here. That is why I developed an AI Governance Package for the mortgage broking industry, and why this work remains central to my career direction.

Aboriginal Justice

My legal studies have given me a deeper understanding of how Australia's legal and governance systems have shaped and continue to shape outcomes for First Nations people. The history of the Stolen Generation, the collision between traditional law and Western legal frameworks, the ongoing effects on incarceration rates these are present realities. This has become an important and humbling part of my broader worldview on what systemic fairness actually requires.

"I define success by the impact I have on people's lives no matter how small the change, no matter how long it takes."
Stacey Dragicevich

Personality

Understanding who I am and how that shapes the work I do best

I have always known I was drawn to complex problems, to people, and to the idea that systems, when designed well, can genuinely change outcomes. What I did not fully understand, until I worked through the career and personality assessments in this module, was how precisely those instincts mapped to my personality profile.

The foundation for effective career development is having a strong understanding of your vocational identity (Brown & Hirsch, 2008). One way to predict consistent career success is through understanding where you sit in the Big Five personality traits (Heslin et al., 2019). Holland's (1997) RIASEC model puts these two together to determine occupational fits in a practical way.

What follows is my honest reflection on what these assessments revealed including: what surprised me, confirmation of what I already suspected, and what I intend to do differently as a result.

My Big Five Personality Profile

Openness to Experience

Score: 96% (Average: 58%)

My results show a high score in Openness to Experience. I connect ideas across seemingly unrelated fields, approach problems creatively, and am genuinely curious about how complex systems function. This creativity score was higher than I expected, and it helped me understand why purely operational roles feel draining I need to be building, creating, and imagining, not just executing. It also explains why I love both marketing and governance equally they are both design challenges at heart.

Conscientiousness

Score: 60% (Average: 55%)

My Conscientiousness sits at a moderate level accurate on reflection. I am highly organised when the stakes demand it. However, when work feels meaningless or misaligned with my values, follow-through suffers. This reinforces that I thrive when the work itself is meaningful, not just when external deadlines force compliance (pun intended).

Extraversion

Score: 69% (Average: 51%)

I score high in Extraversion energised by people, drawn to communication, and at my best when I have a platform to influence and connect. The challenge, as my results make clear, is that I absorb the emotions of those around me. High extraversion combined with high sensitivity means I need to be intentional about the environments I work in. I have become very aware of this and now incorporate tools from my ADHD diagnosis to assist.

Agreeableness

Score: 85% (Average: 63%)

My high Agreeableness reflects genuine empathy and a prosocial drive. This has been both a strength and a vulnerability the quality that enabled me to mentor others effectively and build genuine trust, and also the quality that has, at times, led me to overextend. I have a tendency to take on other people's problems as my own, which means I need to consciously protect my capacity and choose roles where helping is purposeful, not reactive.

Neuroticism

Score: 67% (Average: 54%)

This was the result that gave me the most pause. My Neuroticism score is elevated I experience stress and emotional pressure more intensely than average. What surprised me was not the score itself, but how clearly it explained patterns I had lived without fully naming. Conflict-heavy environments deplete me quickly. To compensate for this, I will prioritise roles in psychologically safe, values-driven organisations and build structured recovery time into my working week.

📄 Big Five Personality Test — Full Results My complete personality profile · PDF · 316 KB Download ↓

What This Means for My Career

The integration between occupational environment and personality type can forecast both satisfaction and performance (Holland, 1997). Holland later refined the concept: one personality type predominates, with secondary types also influential.

My personality profile high Openness, high Extraversion, high Agreeableness, combined with strong analytical drive maps clearly onto roles requiring both rigorous thinking and genuine human engagement. It also makes clear what I should avoid: repetitive operational roles, environments where conflict is normalised, and work that offers complexity without purpose.

For me, culture fit is not a preference it is a professional necessity.

"It is of utmost importance that my work be consistent with my values. I could never be truly satisfied in a job that did not express who I am."

Values & Organisational Values

What I stand for and why it shapes every decision I make

When personal values and organisational values are not aligned it causes frustration, conflict, and disengagement (Lobel, 1992). When values are aligned it can lead to greater satisfaction and increased productivity (Cameron & Quinn, 1999). There are five well-known values shared across cultures: honesty, respect, responsibility, fairness, and compassion (Kidder, 2005).

I did not always have clear language for what I valued in my work. I knew what energised me and what depleted me. I knew the environments where I thrived and the ones where I quietly contracted. What this module gave me was a framework a way to name those instincts, examine them honestly, and use them as a genuine filter for career decisions.

Values are our internal anchors that shape our preferences, behaviours, and priorities at work (Boyle, 2018). The misalignment between our needs and what the working environment provides leads to discontent. This is consistent with my own experience: when my environment aligns with my values, I am engaged, creative, and at my best. When it does not, I literally don't want to be there anymore.

My Eight Key Career Values

Based on Cook's (2022) values activity, these are the eight values that anchor my career identity.

⚖️

Integrity

Acting with honesty, fairness, and ethical decision-making. I need environments where trust and transparency are core expectations not aspirational statements.

🤝

Helping Others & Meaningful Work

I am fulfilled when my work positively impacts people's lives through mentoring, capability building, advocacy, or governance reform. Work without human purpose doesn't hold my attention.

🛡️

Responsibility

Being trusted with ownership, leadership, accountability, and decisions that matter. Responsibility motivates me to perform at my best it is also tied directly to my commitment to governance.

📚

Learning & Expertise

Continuous growth through study, professional development, and strategic thinking. When I stagnate, I disengage.

🏔️

Challenge

I am motivated by complex work that stretches my thinking and requires problem-solving and strategic analysis. When work is too predictable, I lose motivation.

📣

Influence

The ability to shape culture, improve systems, and contribute to meaningful organisational or industry-level change. I feel most energised when I can influence governance and help others grow.

⭐

Excellence

Working in environments committed to high standards, continuous improvement, and effective outcomes. Excellence is not perfectionism it is the belief that people and processes can always be better.

🌿

Flexibility

A work environment that allows autonomy, balance, and the ability to work in the way that enables my best performance. Flexibility is a structural condition for sustained high performance.

My Career Values at a Glance

The values I ranked as least important predictability and change for its own sake are equally instructive. I am not motivated by routine or instability, but by purposeful, meaningful innovation.

Values Reflection

My eight key career values integrity, helping others, responsibility, learning, challenge, influence, excellence, and flexibility form the core of what I need in a role. Values act as internal anchors that shape our preferences, behaviours, and priorities at work, and dissatisfaction arises when these values are not met (Boyle, 2018). When my environment aligns with my values, I am engaged, creative, and at my best. When it doesn't, I literally don't want to be there anymore.

Understanding these values has given me something I did not previously have: a clear filter. I now know that culture fit is not a preference it is a necessity.

Where I Fit: Organisational Values Alignment

Aligning personal values with organisational values is one of the most reliable predictors of job satisfaction and sustained performance (Cameron & Quinn, 1999). The organisations I am drawn to are not simply offering employment they reflect back the values I already hold.

Organisational ValueMy Matching Value
Integrity & Ethical BehaviourStrong personal and professional ethics the foundation of everything I do
Respect, Inclusion & CaringEmpathy and people-first leadership I lead with humanity, not hierarchy
Learning, Curiosity & InnovationContinuous learner and system-builder I seek growth in every role
Accountability & ExcellenceHigh standards and ownership I hold myself and others to what was promised
Collaboration & TeamworkMentoring, capability development, cross-functional leadership

I am closely aligned with organisations that place integrity and ethical behaviour at their core.

I fit deeply in people-first cultures where empathy and inclusion are structural, not performative.

I have a natural cultural fit with organisations that value innovation and continuous learning.

I resonate strongly with environments that expect genuine ownership and accountability.

Organisations that value teamwork and mentoring align directly with how I lead.

Professional Purpose

Why I do what I do grounded in evidence

Today's world involves so much more than skills and qualifications. It requires a growth mindset coupled with your own beliefs and values (Bates et al., 2019). Professional purpose and career identity are grounded in knowing who you are and what you stand for. They drive intentional decision making rather than reactive responses.

My professional purpose has emerged clearly across every assessment, reflection, and career planning exercise I have completed. It is not something I constructed for this assignment it is something I recognised as having always been present. Doing this assignment reinforced the message.

My purpose is to create fairness, elevate standards, and empower people especially women by improving the systems that shape their lives.

I live this purpose through ethical governance and compliance work that protects consumers and lifts industry standards through developing women in leadership, designing FDV support frameworks, building compliance frameworks that educate, and mentoring the next generation of professionals in financial services. It shows up in my legal studies, in my advocacy, and in the networks I have built.

Your perception to achieve sustainable and meaningful employment is grounded in your beliefs (Rothwell et al., 2008). For me, that belief is grounded not in hope, but in a clear and evidenced understanding of who I am and what I am capable of.

Claremont Purpose Test Results

57/70
High Level of Purpose
Purpose is a sustained commitment to chasing a long-term goal that is personally significant while contributing to the world in a positive way (Greater Good Science Center, 2024). A score of 57/70 indicates a strong, well-formed sense of purpose. Adults with a stronger sense of purpose tend to have sharper cognitive function, lower stress, and longer lifespans purpose shapes how people decide, persevere, and handle challenges (Hill & Turiano, 2014). My score confirms what my values work and personality assessments also reflect: my purpose is already active, not aspirational.

How My Purpose Aligns With My Work

Empowering Women & Building Capability

My previous work through She Knows (133+ members nationally), mentoring, and leadership development programs contributes daily to improving the professional lives of women in financial services.

Ethical Governance & Consumer Protection

My compliance frameworks, investigations, and governance programs are built to protect people from consumers and workers to the industry as a whole.

Mental Health & Workplace Safety

My advocacy for better support systems in mortgage broking and my FDV program design reflect a purposeful commitment to reducing harm in workplaces.

Education, Advocacy & Continuous Learning

My law studies, LinkedIn newsletter, AI Governance Package, and public writing are all expressions of a purpose that extends beyond my immediate role.

📄 Purpose in Life Quiz — Full Results My Claremont Purpose Score results · DOCX · 16 KB Download ↓

Career Identity

Who I am professionally shaped by experience, pointed toward purpose

Career satisfaction and wellbeing are grounded in career identity (Praskova et al., 2015; Stringer & Kerpelman, 2010). Through planning, exploration, decision-making, experience, and substantial reflection, our career identities are developed (Bates et al., 2019). Utilising Gander's (2023) learning cycle grounded in Kolb's (1984) experiential framework career identity develops through four modes: experiencing, reflecting, thinking, and acting.

For me, this module has been an exercise in making explicit what was already forming beneath the surface. I experienced the consequences of poor governance systems. I reflected on what those experiences meant. I began thinking more formally through my law degree. And I acted designing frameworks, advocating for change, building networks.

My career identity now sits at a clear intersection. It centres on governance, compliance, and women's leadership three domains that, when combined, describe not just what I do, but who I am professionally.

My Career Identity as a Millennial

Millennials have delayed exploration of career identity until graduation. Their career identity has evolved over time: starting their journey with expectations that were sometimes low, or when they attempted to enact their ideal career identity they were unprepared, or their experience jarred them. Through time and progression they have extended their learning through experience, intrinsic motivation, and fluctuations in self-awareness rising in traits such as boundaryless, protean, and intrinsic motivations. Rather than succumbing to their environment as previous generations may have done, they choose to bolster their idealism. Identity has become fluid, resulting in mini cycles of growth and exploration where development is ongoing and cyclical (Boyle, 2022).

As a millennial myself, I resonate deeply with this. While my own journey did not follow a traditional graduate pathway, this pattern is me. My most significant career identity shifts have come not from planning, but from confronting moments of misalignment when organisational culture conflicted with my ethics, when compliance failures caused real harm to real people, when I watched capable women leave industries that had not made room for them. My own identity is fluid. The older I have gotten, the more idealistic and determined I have become.

Governance and leadership in action

My Career Identity Venn Diagram

The intersection of Governance, Compliance, and Women's Leadership where my career identity lives

This diagram reflects three workplace preferences that consistently emerge across all my assessments. I need work that is values-driven and people-focused. I need intellectual challenge, creativity, and variety. And I need high social interaction paired with genuine autonomy. Where these three conditions meet that is where I do my best work and find the most meaning.

My Career Identity Statement

"I am an ethics-driven, purpose-centred leader whose work is anchored in integrity, empowerment, and continuous learning. I thrive in roles that provide challenge, autonomy, and the opportunity to influence organisational systems, uplift professional standards, and create meaningful change.

My career identity centres on developing women in leadership, building ethical governance frameworks, supporting mental health and safety, and translating complex compliance and legal requirements into practical, accessible tools. I am at my best in environments that value excellence, flexibility, innovation, and social impact.

My long-term vision is to use my skills in governance, law, leadership, and advocacy to shape policy, improve industry culture, and contribute to building safe, ethical, high-performing organisations across the public, private, or regulatory sectors."
Stacey Dragicevich

The work that matters most — developing others

The Importance of Values in Career Identity

When our values do not align with our career identity it leads to stress, burnout, and dissatisfaction (Tennant, 2001). Reviewing my own values against career theory using the framework of the Protean Career (Hall, 1976) and the Boundaryless Career (Arthur, 1994) reveals a clear picture of how my identity has formed.

ValuesProtean CareerBoundaryless Career
How is achievement definedProfessional commitmentSkills utilisation
What is comfortWork-life balanceWork-life balance
What defines statusPersonal developmentCareer development
Prosocial behaviourMeaningful workMeaningful work
What is a challengeLearning and growthSkill development

My career follows what Hall (1976) describes as a protean orientation self-directed, guided by personal values rather than organisational hierarchy, and reconfigured continuously through experience and reflection. As I have gotten older and my life has changed, it is now a combination of protean and boundaryless. My career has not followed a straight line. It has been a deliberate and ongoing process of exploration, boundary experiences, revision, and recommitment to purpose exactly as Boyle (2022) describes in her research on Millennial career identity development.

The ongoing process of building career identity

Career Aptitude Test: The Inquisitive Idealist

Career Personality Type: Inquisitive Idealist

The Career Aptitude Test describes me as an Inquisitive Idealist — someone motivated by meaning, authenticity, intellectual discovery, and a desire to contribute to the world in a positive way. I am drawn to complex issues, particularly those with a personal or moral dimension, and motivated by both logic and deep empathy.

The profiler identifies my strengths as imagination, forward-thinking, persistence, sociability, altruism, and analytical problem-solving. These are the same qualities that have defined my career over 20 years. The difference now is that I have a framework to name them deliberately.

Speaking out — a core part of who I am

My RIASEC Profile: Holland Code IASE

Based on my Career Aptitude Test results and the Holland (1997) RIASEC framework, my vocational profile is Investigative–Artistic–Social–Enterprising (IASE) — a combination most commonly found in policy makers, governance and compliance leaders, advocates, regulators, and thought leaders (Hoff et al., 2020). Realistic was not a strong fit, as I do not enjoy manual, repetitive, or tool-based work.

RIASEC TypeWhat This Looks Like for MeAligned Roles
Investigative Primary FitLoves analysing complex systems, researching, theorising, working with abstract ideas, understanding how laws, regulations, and organisations function. Thinking score = 100.Regulatory policy analyst, Compliance specialist, AI governance analyst, Governance advisor
Artistic Secondary FitEnjoys creating concepts, designing ideas, using imagination, communication, content creation, advocacy messaging. Creativity score = 98.Advocacy campaign designer; Compliance education designer; Speaker; Writer/editor in governance; Communications strategist
Social Major FitStrong prosocial motivation and empathy; values helping others, advocating, mentoring, capability building, and improving women's outcomes.Women's leadership coach; Industry trainer; Capability development lead; Community advocate; Consumer-protection educator
Enterprising Major FitEnjoys leading change, influencing culture, public speaking, advocacy, and championing regulatory uplift. Persuading score = 84.Compliance leader; Governance executive; Policy advocate; Industry spokesperson; Women-in-finance advocate
Conventional Supporting FitSkilled in organising, structuring information, understanding regulation, analysing data and processes, ensuring accuracy. Organising score = 80.Compliance manager; Governance officer; Policy writer; Regulatory operations; Risk officer

Building the community I wish I had earlier in my career

My Career Capital

Tomlinson's (2016) capital model identifies the resources you have available now that can assist you in building your career. Here is how my five capitals map:

Type of CapitalWhat It Focuses OnWhy It Is Important
HumanKnowledge, technical skills, subject expertiseFoundational — my law degree and 20+ years in compliance strengthen this continuously
SocialNetworks, weak ties, labour market accessBuilds confidence and opportunity — my LinkedIn presence, She Knows network, and industry relationships are strong here
CulturalUnderstanding and adapting to organisational cultureHelps graduates fit expectations — I understand governance culture deeply across sectors
PsychologicalResilience, adaptability, coping with adversityHelps manage setbacks and risk — this is my primary development area
IdentityCareer identity, future self, career orientationSupports employability and recruitment success — my career identity is clear and evidence-based

Strongest Capitals

Social Capital — networking, confidence, online presence, opportunity recognition

Human Capital — academic knowledge, transferable skills, recruitment performance

Strong but Still Growing

Cultural Capital — understanding sectors and cultural awareness across industries

Main Development Priority

Psychological Capital — adaptability, resilience, stress management, risk planning, and career contingency planning

Translating complexity into clarity — every time

Motivation & Meaningful Work

What drives me, what sustains me and what I have learned about both

Understanding what motivates me feels less like theory and more like a practical lens for navigating my career. The distinction between intrinsic and prosocial motivation clarified why meaningful work for me is tied so closely to values and identity. When my work aligns with these, my energy and sense of purpose naturally strengthen.

Working through the motivation frameworks was unexpectedly revealing. I found myself not just analysing the concepts but applying them to the tensions and decisions I am facing right now. The process was both clarifying and confronting, because it highlighted what genuinely drives me and what no longer does.

Prosocial Purpose Over Extrinsic Reward

Theory — Grant (2008)

  • Prosocial motivation = the desire to expand effort to benefit other people
  • Intrinsic and prosocial motivation are not competing forces
  • Together they strengthen persistence, performance, and productivity
  • Meaningful work connects values, identity, and contribution to something larger

When both are present, people show greater persistence because the work feels personally meaningful and socially impactful.

Application — My Experience

  • My motivation has never been driven by salary, status, or title
  • Work feels meaningful when I can see who it supports and why it matters
  • The value is rarely in the task itself — it is in the impact on people
  • I am energised by intellectual complexity and sustained by purpose

When engagement drops, I no longer interpret that as a flaw. It is simply data — a signal about the environments where I thrive.

Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory

Satisfaction and dissatisfaction stem from two different sources. Motivators create genuine fulfilment, while hygiene factors simply prevent dissatisfaction their presence alone is not enough to make work meaningful (Herzberg et al., 1959).

Motivators What Creates Fulfilment

  • Meaningful work
  • Responsibility and ownership
  • Recognition for contribution
  • Intellectual growth
  • Achievement that matters

When I am building a compliance framework, mentoring, or shaping an advocacy program, I am fully in the motivator space. In those moments, the work is inherently rewarding.

Hygiene Factors What Can Erode

  • Contract and remuneration
  • Task clarity and scope
  • Organisational structure
  • Working conditions
  • Role definition

A contract and a full task list don't create meaningful work. They keep you showing up but they don't create the desire to stay.

Adams' Equity Theory

People evaluate fairness by weighing what they put into their work against what they receive in return and when this balance feels off, motivation and performance naturally decline (Adams, 1976).

My contributions of two decades of expertise, initiative, intellectual property, loyalty, and genuine care need to be met with proportional recognition, compensation, and respect. When that balance is persistently missing, the impact goes beyond day-to-day motivation. Over time, inequity doesn't just drain energy it starts to unsettle identity. What this framework clarified for me is that choosing the right environment is fundamental, not a preference.

Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs: A Personal Map

Maslow (1943) — a framework I have not just studied, but inhabited.

Human needs go from basic physiological survival to safety, belonging, esteem, and ultimately self-actualisation the space where we strive to become our fullest selves (Maslow, 1943). The model is often depicted as a simple upward progression, but my experience suggests something far more fluid a dynamic map of shifting human needs shaped by context, circumstance, and the choices we make (Gibson, 1994).

Looking back over my career, I can see how I moved through Maslow's hierarchy. Early on, my focus was on the fundamentals income, stability, finding my place in an industry that wasn't easy to break into. As my expertise grew, so did my sense of belonging and esteem, grounded in the teams I built and the work that genuinely mattered.

There were also moments creating a compliance framework, launching She Knows, watching someone step into business ownership because of something I helped create where I touched self-actualisation and felt completely aligned with the work I was meant to do.

But the hierarchy isn't fixed. Certain environments can pull you back down. When safety is compromised, when contributions are taken rather than valued accessing those higher levels becomes impossible. I haven't always been operating from purpose; at times, I've been managing survival.

And the reality is practical as much as personal. I have children who rely on me, a family to support, a mortgage that doesn't pause. Maslow's safety needs are not theoretical for me they show up in everyday responsibilities.

What has shifted is my response. I am building my own compliance framework shaped entirely from my own expertise to take to market independently. This is a deliberate move to rebuild upward on my own terms: safety through ownership, belonging through chosen community, esteem through values-aligned work, and ultimately, self-actualisation through a career that is genuinely mine.

Prosocial motivation, without boundaries, can lead to burnout (Grant, 2008). What I am taking from this period is simple: the environment we choose determines whether meaningful work is sustainable. Moving toward work built on my own intellectual property, aligned with my values, and serving the industry I care about that is not reckless. It is responsible.

"Not a destination. A direction."
Stacey Dragicevich, Motivation Reflection, 2026

Career Direction

Where I am going, how I plan to get there, and who I am becoming

My 80,000-Hour Career Plan

The 80,000 Hours Career Planning process takes its name from a confronting calculation: if you work full-time for 40 years, you will spend approximately 80,000 hours on your career. That is enough time to make a significant difference if the work is chosen well. Working through this framework gave me clarity not just about what I want, but about the conditions I need, the capital I already hold, and the plan I am prepared to execute.

What I Find Most and Least Fulfilling

Most Fulfilling

  • Compliance and regulation policy development
  • Training others in compliance and regulation
  • Leadership developing and supporting women
  • Leading and developing new projects
  • Working in environments with a strong, ethical culture

Least Fulfilling

  • Monotonous tasks with no creative or strategic dimension
  • Environments without a mentor or development pathway
  • Workplace conflict and unclear leadership
  • Roles where I am not given the information I need to do my job well

My Career Capital

I learn quickly and like to know everything about a subject. I am enthusiastic when I care about the work, and I am good at building relationships that matter. I have 20+ years in financial services, twice been a finalist for Thought Leader of the Year, and received an Excellence Award for a business of five or fewer people. I work both analytically and creatively not many people can do both. I have a strong marketing background alongside compliance expertise, and I am finalising my Bachelor of Laws in Business. This is rare career capital, and it positions me well for roles at the intersection of governance, policy, and advocacy.

Global Problems I Think Are Most Pressing

The problems I care about most are not abstract they intersect directly with the work I do and the world my children will inherit:

The Gender Gap in the Workforce

We have a long way to go. The reduction of diversity obligations in large organisations, burnout feminism replacing the lean-in era, women stepping back from career progression, and a persistent pay gap for doing the same job. This is a structural problem, not a personal one.

AI Integration and Governance

Artificial intelligence is reshaping workplaces faster than most regulatory frameworks can keep up with. The risk of harm to workers, consumers, and communities is real and immediate. This is exactly where I want to be working.

Global Economic Pressure

Inflation, global conflict, and the flow-on effects to housing affordability in Australia directly affect the industry I work in and the people I serve. These forces shape financial wellbeing, particularly for women.

Climate Change

I have children. I think about what the world will look like for them environmentally, economically, and politically. This is not a future problem for me. It is a present one.

My Ranked Long-Term Career Options

RankOption
1Women in Financial Services developing support structures for women in their 40s navigating midlife change, mentorship, and advocacy by speaking out
2Industry leader in compliance working from within a compliance organisation to create change, develop consumer protection regulation, and build training cultures
3Speaking at industry events shaping the policy and governance conversation publicly
4Developing technology that assists compliance AI governance tools that are rigorous, human, and accessible
5Achieving genuine work-life balance that sustains the above

My Plan A / B / Z

Plan A

Take a role within an influential government body ASIC, legislative branches, domestic regulation, national security, or policy areas where I can play a meaningful role in the development, enactment, and enforcement of AI governance and compliance. Supported by: completing my Bachelor of Laws in Business, pursuing a graduate program in governance, independent study in AI, and building a portfolio through my compliance newsletter and industry presence.

Plan B

If Plan A proves unsuitable remain in policy and governance development but across a different field. My expertise is transferable. The sector may change; the purpose does not.

Plan Z

If everything goes wrong return to contracting in compliance or find freelance governance work. I have skills that are in demand. The floor is solid, which makes it easier to aim for the ceiling.

My Next Steps

  1. Use LinkedIn network actively not cold applications, but warm connections and referrals
  2. Set up LinkedIn job alerts for target roles in governance, compliance policy, and AI regulation
  3. Build a portfolio of work that demonstrates my expertise publicly
  4. Continue my compliance newsletter and track what gains traction
  5. Talk to industry bodies MFAA, FBAA, and governance associations
  6. Pursue post-graduate study in governance and an independent AI course
  7. Prepare interview materials: three key messages, concrete stories, and a clear one-sentence offer
  8. Negotiate confidently know my worth and be prepared to name it
  9. Review this plan on 1 July 2026

Plan review date: 1 July 2026. Signs this plan needs revision: not getting interviews for organisations I am targeting, or feedback that signals a skills gap I need to close.

📄 80,000 Hours Career Plan — Full Document My complete career planning responses · DOCX · 20 KB Download ↓

What I Will Not Give Up

Career planning is not just about what you want it is about what you are not willing to compromise.

Ethical Standards & Governance Integrity

I will not work in environments that expect me to compromise on ethics, transparency, or regulatory accountability. Governance integrity is foundational not negotiable.

Women's Empowerment & Safety Advocacy

My commitment to developing women in leadership, supporting She Knows, and advocating for domestic violence policy is part of my professional identity. No role changes that.

Clarity and Accessibility in Compliance

I insist on translating complex requirements into practical, people-centred tools. Compliance that cannot be understood cannot be followed.

A Culture of Psychological Safety

I need environments where people are trusted, fairly led, and safe to speak. I will not trade a healthy culture for a prestigious title experience has taught me that the cost is too high.

Alignment to the 12 Australian Blueprint Competencies

The Australian Blueprint for Career Development (ABCD) provides a nationally recognised framework of 12 competencies. My current career stage maps to the Advancing phase across most competencies.

A Personal Management
1. Build & maintain a positive self-conceptAdvancing▼

Strong, evidence-backed confidence grounded in 20+ years of results.

Developing: Executive presence under challenge fine-tuning how I hold my ground when challenged in high-stakes settings.

2. Interact positively & effectively with othersAdvancing▼

Strong mentoring, cross-functional collaboration, and conflict navigation.

Developing: Holding my position in the moment without self-censoring.

3. Change & grow throughout lifeAdvancing▼

Led regulatory change, mergers, system migrations.

Developing: Codifying my personal change playbook for future leadership contexts.

4. Manage wellbeing, mental & physical healthGroundwork → Advancing▼

Recognised burnout risk and ADHD factors actively.

Developing: Systematic capacity management and weekly recovery routines.

B Learning & Work Exploration
5. Participate in lifelong learningAdvancing▼

Ongoing Bachelor of Laws in Business, policy writing, research orientation.

Developing: A visible 12–24 month learning plan aligned to governance and AI regulatory roles.

6. Locate & use career information effectivelyAdvancing▼

Skilled use of legislation, regulatory guidance, and labour market data.

Developing: Broader external signals for national policy and public sector pathways.

7. Understand the relationship between work, society & the economyAdvancing▼

I integrate risk, consumer outcomes, regulation, and market integrity across all my work.

Developing: Explicitly incorporating macro trends into my personal career plan.

8. Understand the changing nature of life & work rolesAdvancing▼

Led inclusion initiatives including She Knows, FDV programs, cross-generational mentoring.

Developing: Continued advocacy with measurable outcomes.

C Career Building
9. Secure, create & maintain workAdvancing▼

Manage stakeholder portfolios, create processes and frameworks, negotiate complexity independently.

Developing: Public-facing evidence of employability for target roles including ASIC and governance bodies.

10. Make career-enhancing decisionsGroundwork → Advancing▼

High-stakes decision-making is a strength.

Developing: A repeatable personal decision protocol for career choices offers, secondments, study.

11. Maintain balanced life & work rolesGroundwork▼

Long-standing work-life imbalance acknowledged honestly.

Developing: Translating intention into non-negotiable constraints I will actually keep.

12. Understand, engage in & manage the career-building processAdvancing▼

Actively designing programs, policies, and thought leadership.

Developing: Sharpening my career portfolio narrative for targeted governance and regulatory opportunities.

Dream Job & Organisation: ASIC

The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) represents one of my most aligned target organisations. As Australia's corporate, markets, and financial services regulator, ASIC operates at the exact intersection of governance, compliance, consumer protection, and ethical leadership that defines my career purpose.

ASIC ValueWhat It Means at ASICHow I Demonstrate It
AccountabilityDelivering outstanding results, showing personal drive and resilienceAs Responsible Manager, I achieved 80%+ reduction in audit non-conformities, managed statutory IDR deadlines, and produced Board-ready papers mapping conduct to legislation
ProfessionalismAlways being honest and fair, valuing and treating everyone with respectMy entire career in compliance leadership is grounded in ethical conduct, procedural fairness, and integrity under pressure including managing director-level investigations to Board level
TeamworkWorking collaboratively, supporting colleagues, contributing to shared goalsI founded She Knows, led cross-functional working groups, mentored brokers into business ownership, and designed enterprise-wide training programs that lifted entire teams

ASIC's mandate to make financial markets fair, strong, and efficient is the systemic expression of what I care about most. Working within that mandate would not be a career move. It would be a values alignment.

Future Stacey: My 20-Year Vision

If everything works out as well as it possibly can this is where I am headed.

Leading at the Systems Level

Operating in government, regulatory, or industry body roles not managing on the ground, but influencing at scale. Shaping ethics, governance culture, and sustainability policy for industries that affect millions of Australians.

A Recognised Thought Leader & Advocate

My name is associated with governance reform, AI regulation, and women's leadership. I am invited to speak, to advise, and to shape the policy conversations that matter.

Designing High-Trust Organisations

Building and stewarding institutions that are ethical, resilient, and socially responsible where governance is not a compliance burden, but a cultural foundation.

Still Lifting Women and the Industry

She Knows has evolved into a scalable national ecosystem. I am still mentoring. Still advocating. Still making it easier for the next generation of women to step into leadership.

Bridging Law, Economics, and Practice

Turning complex regulation into simple, adopted frameworks that protect consumers and empower professionals across financial services, AI governance, and beyond.

"I define success by impact not title. In 20 years, happiness looks like visible, lasting improvements in standards, equity, and wellbeing that I helped create."
Stacey Dragicevich

Academic Reflection

"For the first time in a long while, I can feel myself moving decisively in the right direction."
— Stacey Dragicevich

I arrived in this unit with over 20 years of professional and life experience. However, over the last few weeks I have gained more clarity about my career identity than in the 20 years it took to get here. While that was a confronting realisation, it was an important understanding that has fundamentally changed my career direction. Students who engage in early career exploration increase their employability (Artiss, 2019; Bates, 2019), leading to enhanced reasoning, less self-doubt, and greater occupational satisfaction (Praskova et al., 2015). However, most Millennials delay that exploration well after graduating, instead using time, career progression, and life experience to develop boundaryless and protean careers that fluctuate over time (Boyle, 2022).

What Boyle (2022) describes is a pattern that mirrors my own. I entered the workforce thinking I had some idea of what I wanted, but it was not an exploration of identity. That transition was reactive, not intentional (Boyle, 2022). I fell into my first role, progressed quickly, and spent years accumulating expertise before I ever stopped to ask the deeper questions. A misalignment I could feel and disengagement that turned into resentment. I found my purpose the hard way, through self-reflection and experience rather than design. My career has reflected the four themes of identity work restraining, reasserting, revising, and ideal-self re-exploration (Boyle, 2022). As a young woman with a senior role in a male-dominated industry, I restrained my ideal self for years.

Experiencing disillusionment can increase intrinsic motivation (Boyle, 2022), and for me that is exactly what happened, but it took a decade shaped by significant life experiences to get there. Having children changed everything. It recalibrated what I needed, what I valued, and what I was no longer willing to compromise on. Careers unfold through cycles of adaptation and understanding rather than predictable paths (Arthur & McMahon, 2019), and my path reflects that entirely.

Values are the internal anchors that shape our sense of right and wrong (Cook, 2022). Once I understood mine with clarity, environments that violated them became impossible to ignore. The environments I find most stressful share a common pattern: they lack ethical grounding, undervalue contribution, and offer no meaningful impact. These are not preferences they are conditions that directly trigger disengagement and, at their worst, burnout. Prosocial motivation is a core driver of engagement, but without clear boundaries it significantly increases burnout risk (Grant, 2008). I have learned that giving that energy to environments that do not deserve it is not noble, it is unsustainable.

Reflecting through Maslow's (1943) hierarchy brought further structure to this understanding. Early on, safety and stability dominated. As expertise grew, I moved into belonging and esteem. Self-actualisation came later after the moments that forced me to rebuild. I now recognise that environments or circumstances can move me up or down the hierarchy repeatedly. My Claremont Purpose score and my RIASEC profile confirmed what experience had been teaching me. I need complexity, ethical culture, and meaningful impact to stay engaged. The 80,000 Hours framework made conflict, monotony, and environments that do not reflect my values are what drains me.

Going forward I will treat environment as non-negotiable, build boundaries that protect rather than limit my prosocial drive, and continue the structured reflection this unit has introduced. Alvesson (2010) describes identity as an ongoing process of forming, repairing, and revising constructions that produce coherence and distinctiveness (Alvesson, 2010, as cited in Boyle, 2022). My task now is not to discover myself anew, but to act with greater intentionality from the clarity I now have. For the first time in a long while, I can feel myself moving decisively in the right direction.

60-Second Video Pitch

My values, personal brand, and professional purpose

References & AI Declaration

APA 7 references and GenAI acknowledgement

Reference List

80,000 Hours. (2024). Career planning tool. https://80000hours.org/career-planning/career-planning-tool/

Australian Government. (2019). Australian Blueprint for Career Development. Department of Employment, Skills, Small and Family Business.

Bates, E. A., Davis, H. A., & Gander, M. (2019). Professional purpose and career identity: A framework for employability development. Journal of Career Development, 46(3), 261–274.

Boyle, K. A. (2022). Career identities and Millennials' response to the graduate transition to work: Lessons learned. Journal of Education and Work, 35(1), 78–91. https://doi.org/10.1080/13639080.2021.2009782

Cameron, K. S., & Quinn, R. E. (1999). Diagnosing and changing organizational culture: Based on the competing values framework. Addison-Wesley.

Gander, M. (2023). Career identity learning cycle. In Developing career identity in higher education. Springer.

Hoff, K. A., Briley, D. A., Wee, C. J. M., & Rounds, J. (2020). Normative changes in interests from adolescence to adulthood. Psychological Bulletin, 146(10), 867–902. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000245

Access, Submission & GenAI Declaration

I used generative AI tools to support, not complete this assignment, in the following ways:

Claude (Anthropic) Pro Plan was used for technical assistance in building my website. This included advice on how to structure page layouts, create hyperlinks, embed content, and troubleshoot formatting issues. All written content, reflections, and career narrative on the site are my own. I would have used Copilot but Claude was better at helping me with the technical aspects of building the website.

Microsoft Copilot Enterprise Edition was used to review and provide grammatical editing support for my video pitch script. The ideas, voice, and content of the script are entirely my own. Copilot was not used to write or generate the script and assist me to understand when to pause in the script for delivery.

Ideogram (AI image generation) was used to create stylised images for the website, generated from my original professional photograph as a base.

Please note that I own my professional headshot and that is what went into Ideogram as the source.

Please note I tried to use the WIX template and the AI contained in there, but I did not find it suitable for what I wanted to build.

I have 8 drafts of my reflection, 4 drafts of the website outline and 3 drafts of the website copy (each section). The video pitch has gone through 3 drafts.

Reference material was sourced from the references provided in the unit.

All GenAI use was limited to technical, editorial, and visual support. The substantive work especially my career reflection, values articulation, test analysis, and academic writing is all my own.
